raw.regions_all: Suriye; Irak; Mısır; Kuzey Afrika; İspanya; Horasan; Mâverâünnehir; Sind; Ermenistan; Kafkasya
raw.narrative_en: The Umayyad Caliphate transformed the Islamic state from a tribal republic into a centralized empire spanning the Mediterranean to Central Asia. The Damascus-based dynasty, founded by Mu'awiya, consolidated state identity under Abd al-Malik by making Arabic the official language and minting Islamic coinage. While conquests reached Spain and Sind, the mawali grievances and Ahl al-Bayt opposition deepened the regime's legitimacy crisis.

raw.parent_tribe: Quraysh - Hāshim (Hz. Peygamber'in amcası al-'Abbās'ın soyundan)
raw.regions_all: Irak; İran; Suriye; Mısır; Hicaz; Horasan; Mâverâünnehir (başlangıçta); Kahire (sonraki dönem)
raw.narrative_en: The Abbasids built the golden age of Islamic civilization and remained the symbolic center of the Sunni world for five centuries. They founded Baghdad, transforming it into the era's greatest intellectual and commercial hub where the Bayt al-Hikma translated Greek, Indian, and Persian knowledge into Arabic. The reigns of Harun al-Rashid and al-Ma'mun represented the zenith, while from the 10th century real power passed to military dynasties like the Buyids and Seljuks. The shadow caliphate in Cairo served a symbolic function, legitimizing Mamluk sultans.

names.zh: 巴赫瑪尼 - 卡扎爾家族（波斯貴族）

names.en: Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists of Iran
Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization
names.zh: 伊斯蘭革命組織聖戰者
names.fr: Organisation des moudjahidines de la Révolution Islamique

raw.notes: Sayyid clerical family; Muqtada al-Sadr, Musa al-Sadr (Lebanon)
Imam of Nizari Ismailis; Prince Karim Aga Khan IV (d.2025), Prince Rahim Aga Khan V
Sayyid clerical family; Grand Ayatollah Ali al-Sistani

raw.regions_all: Tebriz; Kazvin; İsfahan; Azerbaycan; Horasan; Irak (geçici); Gürcistan; Herat (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Safavids were the dynasty that transformed Iran into a Twelver Shī'ī state, laying the foundations of modern Iranian national identity. Founded through Shāh Ismā'īl's charismatic leadership, the state transformed Isfahan into one of the world's most magnificent capitals under Shāh Abbās. While Ottoman-Safavid rivalry permanently shaped the Sunni-Shī'ī axis of the Middle East, Isfahan's Naqsh-e Jahān Square was built as one of Islamic architecture's most spectacular plazas. The Safavids also institutionalized the Iranian carpet tradition, miniature painting, and Shī'ī theology. The dynasty, which collapsed when the Afghan Ghilzai invasion took Isfahan, permanently determined Iran's political structure.

The Ghaznavids were a Turkic dynasty that gained independence from Samanid ghulam governorship, establishing a powerful empire from Afghanistan to northern India. Sultan Mahmud launched seventeen campaigns into India, initiating the first great wave of Islam's spread in the subcontinent. Patronizing Ferdowsi's Shahnameh and hosting universal scholars like Bīrūnī, Mahmud is one of Islamic history's most contradictory rulers.
The Bānījūrids were a small Iranian dynasty ruling as Samanid vassals in Tukharistan (Balkh region). They lost their independence as Samanid central authority strengthened.

The 'Annāzids were a Kurdish principality ruling in a strategic position near the Iraqi border along the Hulwan-Dinavar axis. Reaching their peak under Abū al-Fatḥ Muḥammad's leadership, this dynasty played a role in regional balances during the Būyid-Seljuq transition period.

raw.capital: Firrim (1. kol); Sârî (2. kol); Amul (3. kol)
raw.narrative_en: The Bāwandid Ispahbadhs ruled in Tabaristan for nearly seven centuries, forming one of the Islamic world's longest-lasting dynasties alongside the Shirvanshahs. Governing in three separate branches, the Bāwandids successfully carried the Sasanian Ispahbadh tradition into the Islamic era.

The Dābūyids were a dynasty continuing the Iranian Ispahbadh tradition from the Sasanian era in the mountainous Tabaristan region. As one of the last Iranian principalities resisting Islam, they ended with the Abbasid army's conquest of the region.

The Hāshimids of Derbent governed Bāb al-Abwāb ('Gate of Gates'), the most strategic pass in the Caucasus, for two centuries, serving as a critical frontier fortress in Islam's northward expansion. Transitioning from Abbasid border governorship to a local dynasty, they defended the Islamic frontier against Khazar and Caucasian peoples.

The Justānids were a local Iranian dynasty governing the late-Islamizing communities in the mountainous Daylam region on the southern shore of the Caspian Sea. Facilitating Islam's penetration into this region, the Justānids can be considered precursors to the Būyids who emerged from the same geography.

The Kākūyids were a dynasty of Daylamī origin that rose in Isfahan and Hamadan as the Būyids weakened. Under 'Alā' al-Dawla's leadership, resisting both Ghaznavid Mahmud and Seljuq Tughril Beg, the Kākūyids continued as a small principality in Yazd after being expelled from Isfahan.

The Sajids, a Turco-Iranian family from Ushrusana, rose through Abbasid service to become a significant regional power controlling Azerbaijan and Armenia. Despite their short lifespan, they represented one of the earliest examples of Turco-Islamic presence in the Caucasus. Yūsuf b. Abū al-Sāj, the dynasty's most powerful emir, played a decisive role in Abbasid politics.

raw.regions_all: Tebriz; Sultaniye; İsfahan; Bağdat; Azerbaycan; Anadolu (tâbi); Gürcistan; Horasan
raw.successor: Celâyirliler; Muzafferîler; Kurtlar; çeşitli yerel hanedanlar
raw.narrative_en: The Il-Khanids were a Mongol state centered on Iran-Iraq-Azerbaijan founded by Hülegü. Having ended the Abbasid caliphate by destroying Baghdad, the Il-Khanids underwent transformation with Ghāzān Khān's conversion to Islam. Under Öljeitü, they left magnificent architectural works in Sultaniyya, creating the most brilliant example of the Mongol-Islamic synthesis.

The Jalayirids were a Mongol-Turkic dynasty that rose centered on Baghdad and Tabriz after the Il-Khanid collapse. Sultan Ahmad Jalayir created a rare artist-ruler figure in Islamic art history as both a calligrapher and painter. Severely damaged by Timur's repeated campaigns, the dynasty was liquidated by the Qara Qoyunlu.

The Qara Qoyunlu were a Shī'ī-leaning Turkmen confederation ruling in eastern Anatolia and Azerbaijan. A culturally brilliant period occurred under the poet-ruler Jahānshāh (pen name Ḥaqīqī). They were destroyed by Āq Qoyunlu Uzun Hasan.

raw.regions_all: Diyarbakır; Tebriz; İsfahan; Fars; Irak; Hûzistân
raw.narrative_en: The Āq Qoyunlu were a Turkmen confederation ruling in eastern Anatolia and Iran, becoming a regional power under Uzun Hasan's leadership. Though Uzun Hasan, who sought a Venetian alliance against the Ottomans, was defeated at Otlukbeli, the Āq Qoyunlu legacy directly prepared the ground for the Safavid rise.
The Ahmadīlīs were a dynasty that established a small atabegate in Maragha and ruled for a brief period. They ended under Khwārazm Shāh and Mongol pressure.

raw.regions_all: Azerbaycan; Tebriz; Hemedân; Errân (Gence); Nahçıvan; İsfahan (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Eldigüzids, founded by a commander of Qipchaq Turkic slave origin and known as the Atabegs of Azerbaijan, seized effective control of the Iraq Seljuqs and defended the Islamic frontier against the Georgians. They remained the region's most powerful political actor until the Khwārazm Shāh Jalāl al-Dīn's conquests.

The Musāfirids were an Iranian dynasty that established strong regional dominance in Azerbaijan through the Daylamī military tradition. Governing in two branches, the Musāfirids experienced their peak under Marzubān b. Muhammad's leadership. Carrying the Daylamī warrior culture into Azerbaijani politics, this dynasty shaped the power dynamics of the 10th-century Caucasus.

The Rawwādids, despite their Azdī Arab origins, gradually became Kurdicized and created a distinctive cultural identity centered on Tabriz, Azerbaijan's most important city. Reaching their peak under Abū al-Hayjā' Ḥusayn, the Rawwādids contributed to Tabriz's historical trajectory toward becoming Azerbaijan's capital.

raw.parent_tribe: 1. Yezîdî Şahlar (Arap kökenli iddia); 2. İkinci Şah soyu
raw.narrative_en: The Sharwān Shāhs ruled for nearly eight centuries in the Eastern Caucasus, forming one of the Islamic world's longest-lasting dynasties. Transforming from Sasanian border governorship to an Islamic principality, the dynasty left an architectural and cultural legacy in the Shirvan region. The Palace of the Shirvanshahs in Baku remains the most visible evidence of this tradition.

raw.regions_all: Hicaz; Suriye; Filistin; Mısır; Irak; İran; Bahreyn; Umman; Yemen
raw.narrative_en: The Rashidun Caliphate represents the founding political order of the Islamic community after Prophet Muhammad's death, dismantling the Sasanian Empire entirely and expelling Byzantium from Syria and Egypt within three decades. Abu Bakr secured Arabian unity through the Ridda Wars, while Umar transformed the conquests into a systematic state apparatus. Uthman standardized the Quranic text, but the civil strife (fitna) under Ali created the defining political fracture in Islamic history.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Carmathian or Qarmati Rulers of the line of Abū Sa'īd al-Jannābī
raw.regions_all: al-Ahsā; Bahreyn; Güney Irak (geçici); Suriye sınırı (geçici); Umân (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Qarmatians represented the most radical branch of Ismaili Shiism, a militant movement that shook the Islamic world by stealing the Black Stone from the Kaaba. Their egalitarian community model established in eastern Arabia, while controversial, constituted one of the most provocative experiments in Islamic political thought.

raw.regions_all: Umân; Maskat; Zengibar; Doğu Afrika kıyıları; Bahreyn (geçici); Bender Abbas (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Al Bu Said have governed Oman from the 18th century to the present, establishing a powerful maritime trade empire in Zanzibar. Under Sultan Qaboos (1970-2020), a modern state was built with oil revenues.

raw.regions_all: Horasan; İsfahan; Bağdat; Suriye; Kirmân; Mâverâünnehir (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Seljuqs represent one of the most decisive turning points in Islamic history and the founding force of the Turco-Islamic synthesis. Descending from the Qınıq clan of the Oghuz Turks, the Seljuqs defeated the Ghaznavids at the Battle of Dandanaqan (1040), dominated Khurasan, and within fifteen years took Būyid Baghdad, initiating the resurgence of Sunni Islam. Tughril Beg received the title 'Sultan of the East and West' from the Abbasid caliph, permanently institutionalizing the sultanate in Islamic politics. Alp Arslan's victory at Manzikert (1071) opened Anatolia to the Turco-Islamic world, while the era of Malik Shāh and his vizier Niẓām al-Mulk constituted one of the most brilliant administrative periods in Islamic history. The Niẓāmiyya madrasas institutionalized the Islamic educational system, building the intellectual infrastructure of Sunni orthodoxy. Branches established in Iraq, Kirman, Syria, and Anatolia under the Great Seljuq umbrella redrew the political map of the Islamic world.

raw.regions_all: Granada; Málaga; Almería; kısmen Cádiz, Jaén, Murcia
raw.narrative_en: The Nasrids ruled Granada for two and a half centuries as the last bastion of Islamic al-Andalus, constructing the Alhambra Palace as one of the finest masterpieces of Islamic architectural art. They survived through masterful balancing between Christian Spain and Marinid Morocco, but internal succession struggles and the Castile-Aragon union rendered resistance futile, leading to their surrender in 1492.

raw.regions_all: Semerkand; Herât; İsfahan; Şîraz; Bağdat (geçici); Hindistan (geçici); Anadolu (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Timurids were the dynasty founded by Timur from the Barlas Turco-Mongol clan, transforming Samarkand into a world capital. Timur fundamentally redrew the political map of the Islamic world through military campaigns stretching from China to India, from Russian steppes to the Mediterranean. Yet the true Timurid legacy lies in cultural rather than military achievements: Ulugh Beg's Samarkand observatory broke new ground in astronomy, while the Herat miniature school represents the pinnacle of Islamic art. The era of Husayn Bayqara and Alī Shīr Navā'ī is the golden age of Chagatai Turkish literature. The Timurid cultural legacy was carried to India through the Mughals and to Iran through the Safavids.

Mysore's Muslim rulers Hyder Ali and his son Tipu Sultan led the last great resistance against British colonialism in southern India. Tipu Sultan, nicknamed the 'Tiger of Mysore,' was a pioneer in military use of rocket weapons, dying in battle during the 1799 Siege of Seringapatam.

raw.regions_all: Meşhed; İsfahan; Delhi (geçici sefer); Buhara (geçici sefer)
raw.narrative_en: The Afshārids were a short-lived Iranian dynasty founded through Nādir Shāh's extraordinary military genius, entering history with the enormous booty of the Delhi campaign (Koh-i Noor, Peacock Throne). The empire rapidly disintegrated after Nādir Shāh's assassination.

The Lu'lu'ids were a unique dynasty founded by a commander of Armenian slave origin who governed Mosul alone for thirty years. Badr al-Dīn Lu'lu', as an art patron ruler inheriting the Zangid legacy, preserved Mosul's cultural richness.

The Tāhirids, a family of Khuzā'a origin who rose through Abbasid service, formed the first effectively independent Iranian dynasty. Contributing to the development of Islamic culture in Khurasan while also running the shurta (police) organization in Baghdad, this dynasty maintained an autonomous administration while remaining loyal to the Abbasids.

The Musha'sha'ids were an Arab dynasty founded with extreme Shī'ī ideology in the Khuzestan region of southwestern Iran, ruling for 290 years under the protection of marshland geography. They maintained their existence for a long time as Safavid vassals.

The Zands were a short-lived dynasty of Lur origin that established dominance in southern Iran under Karīm Khān's leadership. Using the title 'Wakīl al-Dawla' (regent of the state) instead of 'Padishāh,' Karīm Khān made Shiraz his capital, maintaining a populist and relatively peaceful governance.

raw.regions_all: Bağdat/Irak; Fars (Şîraz); Cibâl (Rey, Hemedân, İsfahan); Kirmân; Hûzistân; Umân (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Būyids represent the most paradoxical dynasty in Islamic history, emerging from the Daylam mountains on the Caspian coast to conquer Baghdad and directly control the Abbasid caliph. Rather than destroying the Sunni caliphate, this Shī'ī military power pragmatically utilized it as a legitimacy instrument, creating a unique model in Islamic political tradition. Governing in five branches across Fars, Iraq, Jibal, and Kirman, they maintained dominance for over a century. Transplanting the Daylamī military tradition to the heart of the Islamic world, they also played a pioneering role in literary and scientific patronage.

raw.regions_all: Musul; Halep; Şam; Sincar; Cezîre; Şehrizor
raw.narrative_en: The Zangids were a Turkic dynasty that rose from Seljuq governorship and pioneered the jihad movement against the Crusaders in the Islamic world. Ruling in five branches across Mosul, Aleppo, Damascus, and the Jazira, Nūr al-Dīn Maḥmūd was the most important Zangid ruler who transformed jihad ideology into a systematic state policy. Saladin rose through Nūr al-Dīn's service, inheriting the Zangid legacy.
The Atabegs of Yazd were a dynasty of Daylamī origin continuing the Kākūyid legacy as a small local principality in Yazd. They were taken over by the Muzaffarids during the Il-Khanid period.

The Begtiginids were a Turkic dynasty that established an atabegate in Erbil as Zangid vassals and played a significant role in the struggle against the Crusaders. Muzaffar al-Dīn Gökbörü became renowned for fighting alongside Saladin and as the greatest patron of the Mawlid celebration tradition.

The Bādūspānids ruled for approximately nine centuries in the Rūyān region of western Tabaristan, forming one of the Islamic world's longest-lasting dynasties. This mountain principality, with pre-Sasanian Iranian roots, witnessed the rise and fall of numerous empires thanks to the advantage of geographic isolation.

The Hazāraspids were a Kurdish/Lur atabegate that ruled in Greater Luristan for over 270 years. As one of the rare dynasties that survived as Il-Khanid vassals, the Hazāraspids maintained their local authority in Luristan's mountainous geography.

The Shāh-i Armanids were a Seljuq frontier principality that consolidated Turco-Islamic presence in the region by centering on the strategic city of Ahlat around Lake Van. Ahlat's position as a major trade center provided the dynasty with economic power, while continuous border conflicts with Armenian and Georgian neighbors persisted.

raw.successor: Selçuklu fethi; ardından Haçlı Edessa Kontluğu
raw.narrative_en: The Numayrids were a small Bedouin emirate ruling in the Harran and Edessa region. Related to the Mirdasids, this dynasty passed into history with the Seljuk advance and the subsequent establishment of the Crusader County of Edessa.

names.en: Bowling and Cue Sports Federation of the Islamic Republic of Iran

raw.narrative_en: The Dulafids were a short-lived Arab dynasty that ruled briefly in the Jibal region under Abbasid suzerainty. They were liquidated by Caliph al-Mu'tadid's policy of re-establishing central authority.

The Hasanūyids were a Kurdish mountain principality ruling in the mountainous Jibal region of western Iran along the Dinavar-Shahrizor axis. Accumulating great wealth under Badr b. Hasanwayh, this dynasty pursued a balancing policy between major powers like the Būyids and Seljuqs.

The Maliks of Nimrūz were local Iranian principalities that ruled in Sistan for over five centuries on the Saffārid legacy. Governing in two branches, these dynasties maintained local stability in Sistan's harsh desert geography.

raw.notes: Deposed in 1979 Islamic Revolution; current claimant Reza Pahlavi (Crown Prince)

raw.narrative_en: The Qutlughkhānids were a Turkic dynasty that ruled Kirman for eighty years as Mongol vassals, notable for the female ruler Turkān Khātūn. They ended with the collapse of the Il-Khanid order.

The Salghurids, as the Oghuz Turkic Atabegs of Fars, fostered a cultural golden age in Shiraz. The period when Sa'dī wrote his Gulistān and Bustān occurred under this dynasty's patronage, and under Abū Bakr b. Sa'd, Fars remained a center of literature and art despite Il-Khanid pressure. The Salghurids were one of the most successful atabegate models in the Islamic world, combining military power with cultural patronage.

The Shaddādids were a Kurdish dynasty that assumed the defense of Islam in the Arrān region, centered on Ganja and Dvīn. Defending the Islamic frontier against Georgian and Armenian Christian forces, the Shaddādids briefly ruled Ani, contributing to the region's Islamic identity.

The Ziyārids were an Iranian dynasty founded by Mardāvīj b. Ziyār of Gīlān-Daylam origin, establishing strong dominance in the Jurjan-Tabaristan region. Mardāvīj, a short-lived but charismatic leader, attempted to revive the ancient Iranian imperial ideal. His grandson Qābūs b. Wushmgīr became renowned as a patron of literature and science, fostering the creation of classic works like the Qābusnāma. The Ziyārids were among the rare Iranian dynasties combining military power with intellectual patronage.

raw.region_primary: Batı Mali; Kuzey Gine; Gambiya; Senegal
raw.regions_all: Yukarı Nijer; Batı Mali; Kuzey Gine; Gambiya; Senegal; Timbuktu (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Keita Kings of Mali built West Africa's largest medieval empire, known to the world through Mansa Musa's legendary pilgrimage. They transformed Timbuktu into one of the Islamic world's important centers of learning; control of trans-Saharan gold trade brought immense wealth.
The Inju'ids were a small dynasty from the Il-Khanid fiscal tradition that ruled briefly in Fars. They ended by being defeated by the Muzaffarids.

The Muzaffarids rose in Fars and Kirman after the Il-Khanid collapse, as the dynasty of the era when Ḥāfiẓ of Shiraz lived. Under Shāh Shujā's patronage, Shiraz continued to be one of the most brilliant centers of Persian literature. They ended through fratricidal conflicts and Timur's liquidation.

The Pahlavis were Iran's last dynasty, founded through Riżā Shāh's military coup. While Riżā Shāh launched a radical modernization and secularization program, his son Muḥammad Riżā Shāh's White Revolution and the 1953 CIA coup constitute the most controversial pages of Iranian political history. The Pahlavi era, ending with the 1979 Islamic Revolution, is Iran's last monarchic age.

The Qājārs were a dynasty founded by the Qājār tribe of the Oghuz Turks, governing Iran for 145 years. Making Tehran the capital, the Qājārs were prominent for modernization efforts during Nāṣir al-Dīn Shāh's 47-year reign. The Constitutional Revolution (1906) was the most important political transformation of this era. They ended with Riżā Khān's coup.

The Börids, a Turkic atabeg dynasty descending from the Seljuq Syrian governor Tutush's line, governed Damascus for fifty years during the Crusader period. Alternating between alliance and conflict with the Crusaders, the Börids disappeared from history when Zangid Nūr al-Dīn took Damascus.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Nizārī Ismā'īlīs or Assassins in Persia
raw.regions_all: Alamut; Rûdbâr; Kuhistan; Suriye (ayrıca bkz. No. 29)
raw.narrative_en: The Nizārī Ismā'īlīs of Iran, known as the 'Assassins,' began when Hasan-i Sabbāh seized Alamut fortress, transforming assassination into a systematic weapon of war in the Islamic world. Governed from a network of impregnable fortresses in the Alborz mountains, this state reinterpreted Ismā'īlī theology through the New Da'wa reform, and the Alamut library became one of the era's important intellectual centers.

raw.regions_all: Hısn Keyfâ (Hasankeyf); Âmid (Diyarbakır); Harput; Mardin; Meyyâfârikîn
raw.narrative_en: The Artuqids were an Oghuz Turkic dynasty descending from Great Seljuq frontier lords, ruling for over three centuries in three branches across southeastern Anatolia. With their rich coinage tradition and fortress building, the Artuqids pursued a strategic balancing policy in the buffer zone between Crusaders and Seljuqs.
The Ilyāsids were a small Iranian dynasty that ruled very briefly in Kirman, ending when the Būyids conquered the region.

The Karts were an Iranian (Tajik) dynasty connected to the Ghūrids, ruling for 150 years centered on Herat as Il-Khanid vassals. Developing Herat as a cultural center, the Karts fell to Timur's conquests.

raw.parent_tribe: 1. Efrîgîler; 2. Me'mûnîler; 3. Gazneli valileri; 4. Anûştegin kolu
raw.narrative_en: The Khwārazm Shāhs ruled for centuries in four distinct branches in the ancient Khwarazm region south of the Aral Sea, with their final branch becoming the Islamic world's greatest military power. While the oldest Afrīghid branch extended to pre-Islamic times, the Anūshteginid branch transitioned from Seljuq vassalage to independence in the 12th century, building a vast empire from Transoxiana to Iran. However, Sultan Muhammad II Khwārazm Shāh's killing of Mongol envoys triggered Genghis Khan's devastating invasion of the Islamic world. Even Jalāl al-Dīn Mengüberti's heroic resistance could not change this fate.

The Saffārids rose in Sistan under the leadership of Ya'qūb b. Layth, the only major Islamic dynasty founder of popular origin, quickly dominating Khurasan. Though the powerful initial Laysī branch was defeated by the Samanids, the Khalafī branch survived for centuries in Sistan.

The Sarbadārids were a popular movement that rose in western Khurasan centered on Sabzavar after the Il-Khanid collapse. With their name meaning 'those who hang their heads on the gallows,' they represented one of the rare social revolt movements in Islamic history. They ended by submitting to Timur.
The Simjūrids were a small military family of Turkic origin serving in the Samanid army, gaining influence in the Kuhistan region of Khurasan. They ended with the Ghaznavid rise.

raw.regions_all: Buhara; Semerkand; Nîşâbûr; Horasan; Mâverâünnehir; Fergana
raw.narrative_en: The Samanids were the first major native Iranian dynasty to rise under Abbasid suzerainty in Transoxiana, architecting the Persian cultural renaissance. They patronized the birth of post-Islamic Persian literature, laying foundations for Ferdowsi's Shahnameh tradition. Transforming Bukhara into one of the Islamic world's most brilliant intellectual centers, the Samanids also prepared the environment that nurtured universal thinkers like Ibn Sina.

raw.notes: Kurdish tribal family; KDP and KRG presidency
Major Lur tribal confederation of Zagros; Khans of Bakhtiari

names.ar: عبد العزيز بن عبد القادر الجيلاني

names.fr: Corps des gardiens de la révolution islamique
names.es: Cuerpos de la Guardia Revolucionaria Islámica
names.it: Corpo delle guardie della rivoluzione islamica
